one.zh: INJUNCTIONS – Where the mother seeks injunctions restraining the father, the father’s current partner and a third party from communicating to the publ…
one.en: INJUNCTIONS – Where the mother seeks injunctions restraining the father, the father’s current partner and a third party from communicating to the publ…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – INJUNCTIONS – Where the mother seeks injunctions restraining the father, the father’s current partner and a third party from communicating to the public an account of completed parenting proceedings – Where the father and the father’s partner participated in a podcast made by the third party – Where it is not in the child’s best interests for the information contained in the podcast to be disseminated to the public – Where the injunctions sought will supplement compliance with s 114Q of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) to mitigate the established risk of continuing psychological and emotional harm to the child – Mandatory injunctions made pursuant to s 67ZC of the Act or in the alternative where special circumstances are established to supplement compliance with s 114Q of the Act, for the father, the father’s partner and the third party to remove all material published online communicating to the public an account of the parenting proceedings – Request for the Marshal of this Court and the Australian Federal Police to investigate any breach of s 114Q of the Act – Orders as to costs.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAMPTON J:

By way of an Initiating Application filed on 16 February 2026, Ms Zamorano (“the mother) seeks orders:
(a) Restraining each of Mr Zamorano (“the father”), Ms B Zamorano (“the father’s partner”), and Mr Grabhann (“the podcaster”) from communicating or continuing to communicate to the public an account of proceedings number SYC8469/2024 as to the parenting of X (“the child”), born in 2018, currently seven years of age;

(b) For each respondent to do all things necessary to cause the removal of any communication to the public of an account of those proceedings including but not limited to digital podcast platforms being Facebook, Spotify, Apple podcasts, TikTok and Instagram;

(c) That consideration be given to the referral of the matter to the Commonwealth Department of Public Prosecutions; and

(d) Costs.

On 18 February 2026, the following orders were made:
ON AN EX PARTE BASIS THE COURT ORDERS THAT:
Pending further order and until 4.00 pm on 25 February 2026, the father, [Mr Zamorano] be restrained from doing any act or thing communicating or continuing to communicate to the public an account of proceedings number SYC8469/2024 and forthwith shall do all things as are necessary to cause the removal of any recording that communicates to the public any account of those proceedings from any published platform including but not limited to Spotify, YouTube, Facebook, Apple Podcasts, TikTok and Instagram.
On or before 4.00 pm on 19 February 2026, the mother, [Ms Zamorano] shall cause a sealed copy of the following: -
(a) Her Initiating Application filed on 16 February 2026;

(b) Her affidavit filed on 16 February 2026;

(c) The orders made on 13 March 2025; and

(d) These orders.

to be served on:
i. The father by:
Email at [...];
Text message to [...]; and
Facebook messenger to the account “[Mr Zamorano]”

ii. The second respondent, [Ms B Zamorano] by:
Text message to [...]; and
Facebook messenger to the account “[Ms B Zamorano]”

iii. The third respondent, [Mr Grabhann] by:
Email at [...];
Email at [...];
Facebook messenger to the account “[C Podcast]”; and
Facebook messenger to the account “[Mr Grabhann]”.
Any platform publishing the "[C Podcast]", including but not limited to, Spotify, YouTube, Apple, Instagram, TikTok and Facebook.
Each of the father, the second respondent, the third respondent and any other interested person shall file and serve any Response to the Initiating Application of the mother filed on 16 February 2026, and any affidavit material to be relied upon by 4.00 pm on 23 February 2026.
The proceedings are listed in person before the Honourable Justice Campton at 2.15 pm on 25 February 2026.
(Emphasis in original)
I am satisfied that the mother’s affidavit evidence establishes that she has affected service on each of the respondents to the application pursuant to the orders made on 18 February 2026.
No material has been filed in response by any respondent. No appearance has been made by any respondent today. Exhibit 1, being the affidavit of the mother today, promotes a conclusion that no respondent proposes to attend today or engage in the proceedings. In the circumstances, the matter will be determined in the absence of each of the respondents.

BACKGROUND

The mother and the father married in 2017, separated on 28 November 2019 and divorced in 2022.
On 25 October 2024, the mother commenced proceedings in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) as to the parenting of the child pursuant to Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). The father filed a response in those proceedings on 6 December 2024 seeking different orders as to the parenting of the child.
An Independent Children’s Lawyer (“the ICL”) was appointed. Interlocutory defended determinations were made, including an objection to subpoena.
On 13 March 2025, final orders were made with the consent of the parents and the ICL providing for:
Parental/decision-making responsibility
That the mother shall have sole parental responsibility in respect of all major long-term issues as defined in section 4 of the Family Law Act 1975 in relation to the child, [X], born [in] 2018, and in the exercise of this responsibility the mother shall have sole decision-making authority.

Live-with/time and communication

2. The child shall live with the mother.

3. The child shall spend time with the father at the mother’s sole discretion.
The child shall have communication with the father at the mother’s sole discretion.

Passports and international travel
The mother shall have sole authority to apply for and renew the child’s passports and other travelling documents and is authorised to provide a copy of these orders to any authority required for the purposes of the same.

Restraints
Pursuant to section 68B of the Family Law Act (Cth), the father is restrained from:
6.1. Approaching any place where the mother lives;

6.2. Approaching anywhere the child attends school;

6.3. Approaching [D School];

6.4. Approaching the clinics and/or offices of any mental health treater, medical practitioner, or therapist, upon whom/which the child attends;

6.5. Contacting the child’s school;

6.6. Contacting clinics and/or offices of any mental health treater, medical practitioner, or therapist, upon whom/which the child attends.
The Mother be permitted to approach the NSW Registry of Births, Deaths and Marriages to cause the child’s names to be changed as follows: From [...] to [X].
Pursuant to section 28(5) of the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Act 1995 (NSW), the Registrar register the Children’s names as specified by Order 7.

Context of the current relief sought

On 10 February 2026, the mother was scrolling through her Facebook feed and observed a post on the page “[C Podcast]” directed to an episode due to be released in February 2026. To her observation, the post indicated that the father and the father’s partner had participated in the podcast. The post recorded images of the father and the father’s partner identifying their full names with the following quote:
Family court nearly broke me, it dismantled my life piece by piece. I entered Family Court whole and left fighting to survive. It is a broken system, that favoured her lies from the start. It consumed my family and fractured everything we were.

(Quotation marks removed) (Emphasis removed)
Upon being concerned that the podcaster, Mr Grabhann, had conducted an interview with the father and the father’s partner providing an account of proceedings number SYC8469/2024, the mother instructed her lawyer to write to the father and the father’s partner drawing their attention to the provisions of s 114Q of the Act and requesting the removal of any part of the podcast that might offend the section prior to the podcast’s release. The podcast was released a day earlier than anticipated by the mother in February 2026.

THE LAW

Part XIVB of the Act sets out the restrictions on the publication of information arising from proceedings under the Act.
Section 114N outlines the purpose of the provisions under Part XIVB and provides:
It is an offence to communicate an account of proceedings under this Act to the public, if the account identifies certain people involved in the proceedings.

It is an offence to communicate a list of proceedings that are to be dealt with under this Act to the public, and that are identified by reference to the names of the parties to those proceedings.

A communication is not made to the public if the communication is made to a person with a significant and legitimate interest in the subject matter of the communication that is greater than the interest of members of the public generally.
Specifically:
(a) It is an indictable offence to communicate to the public an account of proceedings which identifies a party to the proceedings, a witness in the proceedings, a person who is related to, or is associated with, a party to the proceedings, or a person who is, or is alleged to be, in any other way concerned in the matter to which the proceedings relate (s 114Q(1)).

(b) “Communicate” includes communication by “broadcast or publication or other communication by means of the internet” (s 114P(1)(d) (emphasis added).

(c) Examples of material which might be sufficient to identify a person in an account of the proceedings includes (s 114Q(3)):

(a) a picture, recording, or physical description of the person;

(b) a name or title that identifies the person;

(c) an address or location where the person resides or works;

(d) details of the person’s employment, paid or voluntary;
(e) the relationship or other connection between the person and an identified person or business;

(f) the person’s political, philosophical or religious beliefs;

(g) any real or personal property associated with the person.
(d) A communication is not a communication to the public if the person or body has a “significant and legitimate interest in the subject matter of the communication” and “that interest is substantially greater than, or different from, the interests of members of the public generally” (s 114S).
The Full Court in Xuarez & Vitela [2012] FamCA 574 said (at [35]–[36]), in relation to the former s 121 of the Act, but equally applicable to the current s 114Q:
Clearly, proceedings for an offence against s 121 must be heard on indictment and tried by a jury and can only be commenced by or with the written consent of the Commonwealth Director of Public Prosecutions.
This Court does not itself prosecute or try persons who might be considered to have contravened s 121... At best, the Court can direct the Marshall of the Court and/or the Australian Federal Police to investigate and take steps to ensure any breach of s 121 of the Family Law Act is prosecuted. See Prentice & Bellas and Anor [2012] FamCA 108 per Murphy J.
Section 114T of the Act now provides that for any offence against s 114Q(1) the “written consent of the Director of Public Prosecutions” is required.

The power to make the orders sought

The mother’s application invites consideration of ss 67ZC, 68B, 114, and 114Q of the Act.
The mother said that she did not seek to vary the s 68B orders made on 13 March 2025 having regard to the provisions of s 65DAAA of the Act.
Section 114(3) is relevant only if there are other substantive proceedings on foot in this forum, it being an ancillary injunctive power. Some question exists as to whether the injunctions as sought would fall on all fours within s 114(1) of the Act. That said, s 67ZC of the Act provides the Court with a broad jurisdiction to make orders relating to the welfare of the child. I am satisfied for the purposes of this application that the provisions of s 67ZC are engaged and that the paramount consideration for the making of any injunctive order for the benefit of the child will have at its heart a paramount consideration as to the promotion of the best interests of the child.
In the event I am in error as to that jurisdictional foundation for the making of the orders, the Full Court in Sitwell & Sitwell [2014] FamCAFC 5 (“Sitwell”) considered circumstances which might enliven the Court making an injunction to prevent the publication of an account of proceedings as contemplated by the former s 121 of the Act, now s 114Q of the Act. The Full Court held (at [65]):
(a) Where the proposed injunction is merely a restatement of the effect of s 121 of the Act it is “unnecessary and undesirable” for the Court to make such injunction, the rationale for this is that it was not intended by Parliament that this Court enforce the provisions of s 121 by way of contempt or otherwise (at [35] and [37] citing Gibb & Gibb [1978] FamCA 8; (1978) FLC 90-405 at 77,083 and Re Schwartzkopff (1993) FLC 92-381 at 79,950);

(b) “Special circumstances” must exist if such an injunction is to be made; and

(c) What might constitute a “special circumstance” is not closed but might include that the feared published material and/or published material being likely to impact the welfare or best interests of children, or being a “scandalous disparagement of courts or judicial officers which is likely to impair their authority, or serious and baseless attacks on the integrity or impartiality of courts or judicial officers”.

There is no evidence of proceedings being commenced for a breach of s 114Q or for contempt arising out of the facts complained of by the mother.

CONSIDERATION

The podcast included an engagement by the father and the father’s partner by way of an interview for about two and a half hours. I find that included, amongst other things:
(a) Identification of the father, the father’s partner and the podcaster;

(b) Identification of the child by way of year of birth;

(c) Identification of the mother;

(d) Identification of the orders sought by the mother in the proceedings;

(e) Identification of the father’s partner’s participation in the proceedings;

(f) Identification of the subpoena issued at the request of the mother in the proceedings;

(g) Identification of an interlocutory hearing of the proceedings, determination of the proceedings and the costs order made in the mother’s favour therein;

(h) Identification of the mother’s prior employment;

(i) Identification of the mother’s cultural background;

(j) The child’s change of name; and

(k) Specified references to the mother’s father-in-law being a public servant in Suburb E.

The podcast was published on Spotify, YouTube, and Apple podcasts. Extracts of the podcast are available on Instagram, TikTok and Facebook. The mother gives evidence, and I accept, that the publications have been viewed thousands of times across these social media platforms including a reposting of it.
On 24 February 2026, the Court’s National Communications Office emailed Facebook, Instagram, YouTube and Google requesting the removal of social media posts as to the podcast. Some of the platforms have advised they are investigating the matters subject of request (Exhibit 2).
It is not necessary nor appropriate for me to make findings as to any breaches of s 114Q of the Act as alleged by the mother. That said, by way of the preliminary observations recorded earlier in these reasons, for the purposes of the injunctive relief sought, the facts are suggestive that elements of s 114Q of the Act could be made out.
I accept the mother’s evidence that:
(a) She found the podcast extremely distressing, including the descriptions of her by the father and the father’s partner, and their minimisation of her experiences in the prior proceedings.

(b) A parent of a child at the child’s current school has identified the mother from the information disclosed on the podcast despite the mother not disclosing the father’s name or any aspect of the proceedings to the said parent.

(c) She is gravely concerned that publication of the matters recorded earlier in the podcast and on the digital platforms, will harm both she and the child.

The mother is the person solely responsible for the nurturing and parenting of the child. I accept she has real concerns that the continued or further publication of this information will adversely impact on her parental capacity, including but not limited to, her engagement with others at the child’s school. I accept her evidence and find that information as to the child is being exposed well beyond that to persons that have no proper basis for knowing about him. She expresses concerns that I accept that persons may produce images from the publications to be viewed by the child that would be distressing and harmful to the child. I find that it is not in the child’s best interests for that information to be disseminated.
I accept and find there is an appreciable risk of psychological and emotional harm to the child. I accept and find there is a risk to the mother which will in turn present as a risk to the child, that her role as the sole carer of the child will be adversely impacted by the continued publication of this information. I am comfortably satisfied that for the purposes of s 67ZC of the Act, it is in the best interests of the child to make the injunctive orders sought.
Insofar as it is necessary, I additionally find that the evidence establishes that special circumstances exist of the kind identified in Sitwell for the making of the injunctions to supplement compliance with the law as provided for in s 114Q of the Act, grounded from:
(a) The correspondence and exchanges identified as originating from the father in the mother’s affidavit material promotes a spectre of defiance as to the authority of the Court and as to the obligations imposed by s 114Q of the Act. I find that absent the injunctive orders sought, there is a risk of diminishment of public confidence in the Court and its processes.

(b) It is the case that once information is accessible by way of the internet it may well be that it remains accessible for an indeterminate time in the future having regard to the nature of that facility. That said, notwithstanding what has been published or written can be republished without attribution, the making of the injunctive orders as sought by the mother is not a futile exercise. The injunctive orders as sought are specific and targeted to mitigate the damage that has occurred thus far by the dissemination of the matters identified earlier in these reasons. It is not in the child’s best interests for the information contained within the podcast or portions thereof to continue to be available for dissemination.

REFERRAL

Each of the respondents have had the opportunity to make submissions as to why the documents in this matter ought not be referred to the Marshal to consider an investigation as to any breach of s 114Q of the Act, and if appropriate, to conduct any prosecution of the respondents, and as to the provision of the material to the Australian Federal Police to undertake the same investigation as to whether an indicatable offence has been committed. They have failed or neglected to make themselves available of that opportunity to make these submissions and implicitly they do not wish to be heard as to such referral.
For all the above reasons I make the orders set out herein.

COSTS

The mother seeks an order that the father, the father’s partner and the podcaster jointly and severally pay her costs fixed in the sum of $11,000.
The relevant principles as to costs are well settled. While the starting position established by s 114UB(1) of the Act is that each party pays their own costs, s 114UB(2) allows the Court to make such orders as to costs as it considers just if there are circumstances which justify it doing so. In considering what order for costs, if any, should be made, the Court is required to have regard to the matters set out in s 114UB(3) of the Act and give weight as it considers appropriate to any relevant factor. It is well settled that no single factor in s 114UB(3) has priority, nor must more than one factor be satisfied. Rather, any one factor may be sufficient.
The financial circumstances of each of the respondents are unknown. The mother presents modest financial circumstances, having sole responsibility for the care of the child. The mother has been wholly successful in her relief for the reasons recorded earlier. Her application ought not to have been necessitated. That consideration, in and of itself, is sufficient justification to order costs. That justification is compounded because none of the respondents have engaged in the proceedings to provide any explanation for their conduct. The mother has established that the circumstances justify an order for costs in her favour payable by each of the respondents.
Each of the respondents were on notice that the mother sought costs in her Initiating Application. They have had ample opportunity to consider filing material in relation to costs. They have failed to do so.
The conduct as found of each of the respondent in disseminating the account of the prior proceedings ought not receive a modicum of endorsement. The mother ought not to be out of pocket in seeking to supplement compliance with the provisions of Part XIVB of the Act.
While it is well settled that the Court should not lightly depart from the ordinary rule that costs are payable on a party and party basis, the Court is not restricted to costs being payable on that basis or at the other end of the spectrum, on an indemnity basis. Costs need not be on a scale basis, nor do they need be on an indemnity basis. As identified by the Full Court in Sfakianakis & Sfakianakis [2019] FamCAFC 54; (2019) 59 Fam LR 419, the Court may fix costs in a particular sum taking into account all the relevant circumstances, including the nature of the application and the conduct of the parties in engaging with that application. The Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) permit the Court to adopt a course to fix costs. The fixing of costs avoids the expense, delay and aggravation involved in the assessment process of costs. The touchstone of the fixing of costs requires the Court to be confident that the approach to costs is logical, fair and reasonable.
I have considered the amount claimed by the mother by reference to the material relied upon and the submissions made by her solicitor today as to the charge-out rates for their engagement, and have compared those rates to what would have broadly been applied to the scale as prescribed by the Rules for an application of this nature.
Accordingly, I am satisfied that it is appropriate to fix costs as there is sufficient evidence to make that assessment. Taking into account all the relevant circumstances, I am satisfied that it is appropriate to fix the costs of the mother in the sum of $10,000. This is less than the amount incurred by the mother on an indemnity basis and in excess of what may have been applicable at scale.
It is just to make an order that each of the father, the father’s partner and the podcaster be jointly and severally responsible for the payment of the mother’s costs in circumstances where they collectively have engaged with the conduct identified earlier in these reasons. An order shall be made that each respondent is jointly and severally required to pay the wife’s costs within 28 days.
NOTE:

These reasons have been corrected from the transcript. Topic headings have been inserted, and grammatical errors have been corrected. In addition, amendments have been made to make the orally delivered reasons clear and easy to read.
